name: New Fortress Energy Inc.

profile.description: New Fortress Energy develops and operates LNG supply chains, terminals, and power plants in the Caribbean, Latin America, and Southeast Asia, providing natural gas as a substitute for heavy fuel oil and diesel. Founded in 2014 by Wes Edens, the company pioneered small-scale LNG infrastructure and built the first commercial floating LNG (FLNG) unit. As of June 2026, the company is in active restructuring: two indirect subsidiaries filed Chapter 15 bankruptcy on May 28, 2026, with a restructuring support agreement signed by creditors holding 95%+ of NFE's $5.7B debt, targeting completion in Q3 2026.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): NFE successfully built the world's first commercial floating LNG unit (FLNG 1), placed into service in Q4 2024, and pioneered small-scale LNG terminal infrastructure in markets that had never before had access to natural gas — a genuine category-creating innovation in energy access [1][2]. However, the financial collapse undermines the commercial viability of the innovation. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI deployment or strategy evident; the company is focused entirely on debt restructuring and operational survival [1]. Market Share (1/2): NFE established a pioneering position in Caribbean and Latin American LNG distribution, operating terminals in Puerto Rico, Jamaica (sold May 2025), Brazil, and Nicaragua, though the bankruptcy process puts future market share at risk [1]. Pipeline (0/2): The Brazil terminal and power plant projects were a $470M+ contracted EBITDA pipeline, but these assets are being separated into BrazilCo under the restructuring plan, removing them from the core entity's pipeline visibility [3]. R&D Intensity (0/1): No meaningful R&D spending as the company operates as a capital-intensive infrastructure developer, not a technology company, and all discretionary spending has been curtailed in financial distress [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): NFE's stated purpose — 'Access to affordable, reliable, cleaner energy is not a privilege, but a human right' — is specific, thoughtful, and genuinely service-oriented, connecting LNG infrastructure to economic development, poverty reduction, and environmental improvement in underserved communities [2]. The vision of providing '100% clean energy for everyone, everywhere' and the ZERO hydrogen initiative demonstrate intentionality beyond profit. However, the mission has not been validated by decades of consistent execution — the company was founded in 2014 and is now in bankruptcy after 12 years, falling short of the transformative-and-validated threshold. Community Impact (1/2): NFE's LNG projects measurably reduced air pollution and energy costs in Caribbean and Latin American communities previously dependent on diesel generators; specific impact in Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and Brazil documented through cleaner power delivery [2]. Dollar amounts for community programs not publicly quantified. Employee Retention (0/2): No employee retention data available; the bankruptcy filing and restructuring create an inherently unstable employment environment [1]. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Wes Edens purchased 200,000 shares at ~$8.77/share ($1.75M) on March 11, 2025, and an additional 100,000 shares on March 13, 2025 [5]. Edens has historically invested heavily alongside shareholders, including a $50M personal purchase during a prior equity raise, and insiders collectively own approximately 40% of the company [5]. This signals genuine conviction from the founder, though the stock has declined over 90% since those purchases.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Liquidity check: $0.53 x 10.9M avg daily volume = $5.8M, passes the $1M floor. Q1 2026 revenue was $226.95M vs. $472.28M a year ago, a 52% decline driven by the Jamaica divestiture and commodity price weakness [1]. Net loss of $399.95M in Q1 2026 vs. $178.18M loss a year ago [1]. Forward P/E of 7.54 is the only metric scoring positively, reflecting the deeply distressed valuation and potential post-restructuring earnings power. ROE of -221.5% is catastrophically negative, reflecting accumulated losses against eroded equity. Profit margin of -164.2% demonstrates the company is consuming capital at an unsustainable rate. Revenue growth of -50.8% YoY far exceeds the -20% floor. D/E is not calculable due to negative book equity. The restructuring plan targets reducing debt from $5.7B to $527M, but this will be achieved through massive equity dilution to existing shareholders [3][4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): NFE's core business — LNG liquefaction, regasification terminals, power plants, and physical gas distribution infrastructure — is structurally protected from AI disruption. These are capital-intensive physical assets requiring specialized engineering, regulatory approvals, and geographic positioning that AI cannot substitute [1]. No analyst has flagged AI as a disruption risk for LNG infrastructure companies. The AI-driven data center buildout is structurally positive for natural gas demand, as data centers require reliable baseload power that increasingly comes from natural gas generation — this creates a secular demand tailwind for LNG infrastructure operators [6]. The company's exposure to emerging market energy distribution adds additional physical-world defensibility that AI cannot replicate. The only reason for scoring below IMMUNE (8-9) is that the company's financial distress, not AI, is the existential threat — the business model itself is AI-proof but may not survive its balance sheet. AI Opportunity (0/1): NFE generates no revenue from AI products or services and has no AI deployment strategy [1].

infinite_game.summary: NFE is a textbook case of finite-game thinking producing catastrophic results. Sinek would identify the company's trajectory — $5.7B in debt accumulated through aggressive leverage to fund rapid infrastructure buildout, followed by a going concern warning, missed interest payments, and Chapter 15 bankruptcy — as the terminal consequence of playing to win rather than playing to keep playing. The noble Just Cause of energy access was fatally undermined by financial engineering that prioritized growth speed over durability. Wes Edens' personal conviction and insider buying signal genuine belief in the mission, but Sinek would note that conviction without financial discipline is not courage — it is recklessness. The consensual restructuring with 95% creditor support and the CoreCo/BrazilCo split represent forced adaptation to survival, not the proactive existential flexibility of an infinite-minded organization.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): NFE's cause — energy access as a human right, replacing dirty diesel with cleaner LNG in underserved communities — is genuinely noble, service-oriented, and bigger than any single product [2]. Sinek would recognize the cause as authentic. However, a Just Cause must be resilient and enduring — NFE's cause did not survive its own financial engineering. The company's bankruptcy after only 12 years of operation demonstrates that the cause was not the competitive advantage; it was the marketing. Strategic decisions (accumulating $5.7B in debt, building FLNG on aggressive timelines, expanding into multiple geographies simultaneously) were driven by growth ambition, not by patient, cause-driven execution. Sinek would say: the cause was real, but the company played a finite game in its name.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): A company in Chapter 15 bankruptcy proceedings with two subsidiaries in court-supervised restructuring is, by definition, a low-trust environment for employees [3]. Management disclosed going concern doubt in late 2025, creating existential uncertainty for all staff. The CoreCo/BrazilCo split further fragments organizational identity. While Edens' 40% ownership signals founder-level alignment with shareholders, Sinek's framework asks whether employees feel safe enough to be vulnerable and take risks — that is impossible when the organization's survival is uncertain. The small workforce (approximately 1,200 employees) operating complex LNG infrastructure during bankruptcy creates operational risk as key personnel evaluate their options.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence of an infinite competitive mindset. NFE's competitive positioning was focused on being the first mover in small-scale LNG infrastructure for underserved markets — a valid strategy, but framed as capturing a new market rather than learning from rivals' strengths [2]. The company's competitive language centers on its pioneering role and first-mover advantage rather than on how rivals' capabilities push NFE to improve. In bankruptcy, the competitive lens is irrelevant — survival, not market positioning, is the priority.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The CoreCo/BrazilCo split under the restructuring plan represents a significant strategic transformation — separating the Brazilian assets into an independent entity while restructuring the core LNG business into a 'capital-light, low-leverage business that generates significant free cash flow,' as Edens described it [3][4]. The 91% debt reduction from $5.7B to $527M through debt-to-equity conversion is a dramatic reshaping of the company's financial structure. However, Sinek would note this is forced adaptation under duress, not proactive reinvention in service of the Just Cause. The restructuring was triggered by a missed $163.8M interest payment and going concern warning — this is survival, not flexibility. One point for the scope of transformation; denied the second point because the pivot was compelled by creditors, not chosen by leadership.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): Sinek distinguishes between courage and recklessness. Accumulating $8.9B in total debt (later reduced to $5.7B through asset sales) to fund aggressive global infrastructure expansion is not courage — it is leveraged risk-taking that destroyed shareholder value when it failed [1][3]. The stock declined from $60+ to $0.53, representing over 99% destruction of equity value. Edens' personal $50M+ investment alongside shareholders shows conviction, but Sinek would argue that true Courage to Lead means making decisions that protect the organization's ability to keep playing — not betting the company's survival on commodity prices and project timelines. Missing a $163.8M interest payment and disclosing going concern doubt is the opposite of courageous leadership; it is the consequence of finite-game financial strategy.

investment_thesis: The bull case for NFE requires two beliefs: (1) the restructuring completes successfully in Q3 2026 and (2) the post-restructuring entity retains enough equity value for current shareholders to benefit. The restructuring support agreement, signed by creditors holding 95%+ of NFE's $5.7B debt, targets reducing total debt to approximately $527M — a 91% reduction — through debt-to-equity conversions, preferred stock issuance, and newly structured debt instruments [3][4]. If the CoreCo entity emerges with its LNG terminal assets intact, contracted revenue base, and FLNG 1 operational, the underlying infrastructure has genuine value: Q1 2026 revenue of $227M implies a $900M+ annual run-rate, and the Brazil operations could contribute $470M+ in contracted EBITDA [1]. The Forward P/E of 7.54 reflects deeply distressed pricing that could re-rate dramatically post-restructuring. Wes Edens' restructuring vision of a 'capital-light, low-leverage business generating significant free cash flow' is credible if executed [4]. AI-driven data center power demand creates a secular tailwind for LNG infrastructure.
thesis_continued: The bear case is overwhelming and immediate: NFE's two subsidiaries filed Chapter 15 bankruptcy on May 28, 2026, and the restructuring plan's debt-to-equity conversion will massively dilute existing shareholders [3]. The stock's decline from $60+ to $0.53 (over 99% destruction) prices in near-total equity wipeout. Revenue collapsed 52% YoY in Q1 2026 to $227M following the Jamaica divestiture, with a net loss of $400M — the company is burning cash at an unsustainable rate [1]. Profit margin of -164.2% and ROE of -221.5% reflect a business consuming itself. The restructuring timeline targets Q3 2026 completion but requires U.K. court sanction (hearing June 18, 2026), U.S. Chapter 15 recognition, and shareholder approval — any delay or dissent could collapse the process [3]. Even in the best case, existing common shareholders will own a tiny fraction of the post-restructuring entity after creditors convert $5.2B of debt to equity. Puerto Rico's LUMA Energy is already exploring alternative LNG suppliers in response to the bankruptcy, threatening a key revenue contract [7]. The $113M market cap against $5.7B in pre-restructuring debt speaks for itself.
key_catalysts: Successful completion of the consensual restructuring in Q3 2026 — U.K. court sanction hearing on June 18, 2026 followed by U.S. Chapter 15 recognition could finalize the 91% debt reduction from $5.7B to $527M, dramatically improving the balance sheet and potentially re-rating the equity

CoreCo post-emergence operating performance — if the restructured entity delivers on Edens' vision of capital-light, free-cash-flow-positive operations with FLNG 1, Puerto Rico, and remaining terminal assets intact, the compressed Forward P/E of 7.54 could expand significantly

AI data center power demand boom — the secular increase in natural gas demand from hyperscaler data center buildouts benefits LNG infrastructure operators and could increase the strategic value of NFE's terminal and power generation assets to potential acquirers
key_risks: Existing shareholder dilution in restructuring: the conversion of $5.2B in debt to equity will reduce current common shareholders to a tiny fraction of post-restructuring ownership — even if the company survives and thrives, pre-restructuring equity holders may see minimal recovery

Restructuring execution risk: the plan requires U.K. court sanction, U.S. Chapter 15 recognition, and shareholder approval by Q3 2026; if any creditor group dissents or courts impose unfavorable conditions, the consensual restructuring could collapse into a contested bankruptcy that eliminates equity entirely

Customer contract erosion during bankruptcy: Puerto Rico's LUMA Energy is already exploring alternative LNG suppliers, and other counterparties may invoke change-of-control or bankruptcy-related termination clauses in their contracts, eroding the revenue base that underpins the post-restructuring business plan
